text: The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon protruding through the indirect inguinal hernia. The surgeon applies external manual pressure to partially reduce the herniated sigmoid colon, although a tennis ball-sized portion remains trapped in the groin.

The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon attempting to reduce the incarcerated sigmoid colon within the left inguinal hernia. Despite efforts, the tissue remains firmly lodged and resistant to manipulation.
The robotic system is docked, with the surgeon holding a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and scissors in the right hand, demonstrating that there is no visible left inguinal hernia.

The surgeon injects ten cc's of a quarter percent marking solution into the preperitoneal space near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), resulting in a visible small red dot at the injection site.
The surgeon initiates the creation of a peritoneal flap, opting not to attempt robotic reduction of the bowel due to previous unsuccessful laparoscopic attempts.
The surgeon begins creating a flap, ensuring it is free and working broadly before proceeding further away from the initial area.
The surgeon navigates into the correct plane, staying close to the peritoneum in the Bogros space, aiming to facilitate the dissection of the testicular vessels.

The surgeon identifies the absence of peritoneum laterally, revealing bowel and sigmoid retroperitoneal fat. Careful dissection is performed to avoid damaging the bowel while staying close to the peritoneum.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, revealing a clear differentiation between the tissue associated with the testicular vessels and the tissue belonging to the colon. The transverse salus sling is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon examines the area where the sigmoid colon is adhered, noting the robust structure of the transverse salus sling anchoring the mesh. The surgeon attempts to mobilize the sigmoid by removing attachments from the sling, but observes that the colon remains relatively immobile.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue, avoiding excessive force to prevent tearing. Once the transvascular sling is freed, a small wad of tissue is retracted by the bedside assistant.
The surgeon encounters a portion of the colon that remains adhered, requiring careful detachment of retroperitoneal attachments to the testicular vessels before attempting further reduction.
The surgeon dissects the space of Retzius, attempting to visualize the superior pubic rami, while encountering some adipose tissue in the area.
The surgeon navigates anterior to the retro-rectus fat, encountering a significant amount of adipose tissue despite the patient's thin appearance.

The surgeon observes a significant amount of tissue anteriorly holding the sac in place, which is part of the attachments of the transverse salus sling, transverse abdominus muscle, and transverse salus fascia. These attachments need to be carefully dissected to free the sac.
The surgeon encounters thick bands of scar tissue formed by the chronic sliding of the hernia over decades. These bands are being carefully broken through to facilitate reduction.

The surgeon carefully uses cautery to detach the anterior medial attachments of the hernia sac from the transverse salus fascia, while ensuring the colon remains unharmed and free from thermal injury.
The surgeon continues to take down the attachments, resulting in increased mobility of the hernia sac and colon, facilitating the reduction of incarcerated hernias.

The surgeon carefully separates the testicular vessels from surrounding tissue, using Firefly imaging to aid in identification before potential bleeding occurs. This separation also helps detach the hernia sac and colon, facilitating easier reduction of the hernia.

The surgeon creates a large pocket for the mesh, ensuring good posterior lateral overlap. The testicular vessels are visible, having been separated to prevent them from adhering to the sigmoid colon and becoming entangled in the hernia mass.

The surgeon identifies and divides an anterior vessel, noting its lack of importance, which facilitates the release of surrounding structures. As the attachments are divided, the hernia contents begin to emerge without external pressure.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels and continues dissection along the plane between the hernia sac and the testicular vessels. Scar tissue from the hernia sac is visible, pivoting around the edge.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hernia sac away from the testicular vessels and the vas deferens, ensuring no bleeding occurs by avoiding injury to both large and small vessels.

The surgeon uses cautery to achieve hemostasis, carefully avoiding the vas deferens, which is positioned close to the area being cauterized. The vas deferens is identified and preserved to prevent accidental division.

The surgeon continues developing the pocket by dissecting two centimeters deep to achieve the critical view of the myopectomy orifice, ensuring dissection across the midline by at least two centimeters. The cord is carefully paralyzed during this process.

The surgeon carefully dissects peritoneal structures and bladder fat off the vas deferens, maintaining hemostasis in the process. The dissection proceeds deeply to ensure the mesh can sit behind the pubis and cross the midline. The pubic symphysis is visible on the right side of the body as the surgeon continues dissection into the retropubic space to expose the obturator fat plug.

The surgeon leaves the obturator fat plug in place, prioritizing the core and ensuring good posterior lateral coverage for the indirect hernia repair. The internal ring is inspected for bleeding to prevent a scrotal hematoma. The corona mortis is visible, and further dissection is performed to reach the obturator space. The mesh is positioned between the bladder, vas deferens, and external iliac vein.

The surgeon finishes the procedure by placing a large 3D Max mesh vertically into the retropubic space, ensuring it covers the midline and provides excellent posterior lateral coverage. The mesh is secured with a single 3-0 Vicryl suture to Cooper's ligament, avoiding blindly suturing into the fat. The mesh placement prevents the bladder or hernia from lifting it up.
